title: Local Geo Units in Guyana Need Business Workflows
excerpt: Neighborhood Democratic Councils (NDCs) struggle with revenue collection. Integrating simple GIS workflows can revolutionize how local properties are managed and taxed.
content:
Neighborhood Democratic Councils (NDCs) in Guyana form the backbone of local governance. They are responsible for maintaining roads, drainage infrastructure, and community facilities across hundreds of communities — from the coast to the riverain interior. Yet the majority of NDCs operate with paper ledgers, handwritten rate books, and no digital map of the properties within their jurisdiction. The result is a chronic and worsening shortfall in locally generated revenue that undermines every other function of local government.

The Spatial Gap in Revenue Collection

The core problem is structural: NDC revenue systems are built on lists, not maps. A list of ratepayers can only capture what was already known. It cannot reveal the new house built behind an existing structure on a subdivided lot, the commercial operation running out of a residential building, or the vacant parcel that has sat unregistered since the 1990s. In a country where land use changes rapidly — driven by internal migration, oil-sector growth, and informal settlement — a list-based system falls further behind with every passing year.

A GIS-based approach changes the fundamental logic of revenue administration. When every property has a spatial identity — a geometry tied to a parcel record — the question shifts from "who is on our list?" to "what is on the ground?" This is not a theoretical improvement. It is the difference between a council that discovers new rateable properties reactively and one that can systematically identify and onboard them.

Practically, a spatial workflow allows NDCs to:

  • Visualize compliance on a map — color-coding parcels by payment status (current, in arrears, unregistered) produces an immediate, actionable picture of where revenue is being lost.
  • Identify unregistered properties — by comparing a parcel layer against the rate book, councils can flag structures that appear on satellite imagery but are absent from official records.
  • Plan collection routes efficiently — grouping properties geographically reduces travel time for field officers and increases the number of contacts per day.
  • Detect land-use change — a commercial property assessed at residential rates is a common source of revenue leakage that spatial auditing can surface.

What a Pilot Workflow Looks Like

At GuyNode, we have been developing a proof-of-concept "Business Workflow" layer designed specifically for NDC use. The design principles are deliberate: the workflow must be operable on a basic Android tablet with intermittent connectivity, require no GIS training beyond a half-day orientation, and produce outputs that can be printed or shared as PDFs for use in the field.

The workflow has three components. The first is a parcel base layer — boundary polygons drawn from cadastral data and supplemented with manual digitizing where official records are absent or outdated. The second is a status layer that associates each parcel with one of three simplified codes: Current (rates paid for the assessment year), Arrears (outstanding balance from prior years), or Unregistered (structure or use not captured in the rate book). The third is a field-capture form that allows an officer to photograph a property, confirm its use classification, and flag discrepancies directly from the map interface.

The status layer is maintained as a flat JSON file that can be updated offline and synced when connectivity is available. There is no server-side database requirement. This matters enormously for NDCs in areas where internet access is unreliable.

The Revenue Potential

Conservative estimates from comparable municipalities in the Caribbean suggest that a systematic spatial audit of NDC jurisdictions could increase the registered property base by 15 to 25 percent. In an NDC with 2,000 rateable properties currently on the books, that represents 300 to 500 new revenue sources that have never been formally assessed. Even at the modest rates charged by Guyanese NDCs, the aggregate impact on annual revenue is significant.

The broader argument, however, is not just fiscal. NDCs that can demonstrate transparent, map-based revenue management are better positioned to attract co-financing from national government programs and development partners. A council that can show exactly where its roads are, which properties are paying for their upkeep, and where the drainage backlog is most acute is making a governance argument, not just a financial one.

The Path Forward

The barriers to adoption are not technical — they are institutional. GIS software is widely available and affordable; the challenge is creating a sustained workflow that survives staff turnover and the daily pressures of local government administration. This means designing for simplicity, investing in short but regular training cycles, and building the data maintenance habit into the existing rhythm of quarterly rate assessments.

GuyNode is committed to open-sourcing the workflow templates and base data layers as they mature. Our goal is not to create a dependency on any particular platform but to establish a spatial data standard that any NDC in Guyana can adopt with minimal cost. The data infrastructure for local revenue management should be a public good — and that is exactly what this project is trying to build.
